And so I've got people in coaching who've been with me for 10 years in coaching groups. And what is, as an aside, what is significant about that is they've long since stopped being there for the core reasons of the coaching. They could all conduct a coaching meeting in my absence. So their retention is about something other than that. And it is the something other than that that is pretty important. And the third part of that is, is that all of that goes on with me being very dictatorial. And as one client pointed out, you could make that a play on words. In my relationships with my clients. And so they're staying around and continuing to give me money and asking to give me money more than I will take it. And asking me to do more than I am willing to do with me dictating to them virtually every aspect of the relationship. And being rather difficult to deal with by most standards. Now there's two lessons in that and we'll get to them as we go along. I have, so some people will say, well, you can do that with entrepreneurs, but you can't do it with corporate clients. You can. I have had and have now major corporate clients. I don't seek them. I haven't had a lot of them because they're a giant pain in the rear. But you can do it. So everything we'll talk about in these two days is equally applicable to a Fortune 1000 client. If that happens to be the kind of field you're in, has it is to an entrepreneur client or has it is if you're a high end financial planner or you're a cosmetic dentist or you're a cosmetic surgeon to the individual consumer. You might be sitting across the desk from all of the same principles, all of the same strategies apply equally. And so my clients have included Fortune 5s, Fortune 1s, those giving a million at a time instead of 30,000 at a time. The zeros, the numbers change, the strategies, the arrangements, the management of the client does not change. And so it's very important to get the, my business is different. My client is different. My customer is different out of your head. If at all possible, sometime in the next five minutes so it doesn't get in your way during the next 20 some odd hours. And I got to tell you, everybody in this room conceptually knows that that should be out of your way. Many of you are now turning around and teaching it to people that no, your business is not different. But that doesn't mean you still don't have in your head, my business is different. I did two consulting days this week and one of them I did with people who've been with me for a number of years. And are pretty successful and pretty smart and conceptually know it, intellectually know it. And yet sit there the whole day saying things that reflect a belief system that, yeah, but our clients are really different. And in fact, I will hear off it for people. I know our clients aren't our businesses and different, but and then they will proceed to tell you the five reasons why their business is really different. And so even those of you who are now turning around and teaching to other people that know their businesses really aren't different. It's very easy to have it creep back into your own head about your business or your clients or your situation. And anytime it creeps in, it's self-sabotage, it's in the way. Because what I'm talking about here is absolutely universal. And my career experience kind of proves it. I would point out that these sort of methods work just as well for amateurs and novices as they do for experienced professionals. And probably the best evidence that you can go from zero to 60 in coaching and or consulting without necessarily much background in it is the Glacier Kennedy Independent Business Advisor Program that we created. About half the room here is made up of people who are independent business advisors.
